citations.excerpt: MLDB (Middle Length Discourses of the Buddha) — the canonical English MN10
SuttaCentral MN10 — Sujato's recent translation
dhammatalks.org MN10 — Thanissaro evolved from his earlier 'frames of reference' rendering to 'establishings'

Bhikkhu Anālayo, Bhikkhu Bodhi, and Thanissaro Bhikkhu favor the upaṭṭhāna parsing; traditional Pāli commentary scholars favor the paṭṭhāna parsing. Anālayo argues 'paṭṭhāna' appears only in Abhidhamma texts whereas 'upaṭṭhāna' is readily visible in the Nikayas.

citations.short: Anālayo, Bhikkhu. 'Satipaṭṭhāna: The Direct Path to Realization' (Windhorse Publ…
citations.excerpt: Monograph-length defense of the upaṭṭhāna parse. Chapter 1 contains the philological argument.
The word kha, in later Sanskrit meaning 'sky,' 'ether,' or 'space,' was originally the word for 'hole,' particularly an axle hole, with du- meaning 'bad,' resulting in a bumpy ride analogy.
Collins says the word nirvāṇa is from the verbal root vā 'blow' in the form of past participle vāna 'blown', prefixed with the preverb nis meaning 'out'.
MN10 translation. Thanissaro's signature rendering; emphasises freedom-from-the-aggregates over the cooling-of-fires metaphor.
MLDB MN10. Bodhi preserves nibbāna as a technical term to avoid imported baggage from English equivalents.
SuttaCentral MN10. Sujato recovers the literal blowing-out metaphor; this aligns with his consistent rendering of the three fires as 'greed, hate, delusion'.
MLDB MN10
SuttaCentral MN10
dhammatalks.org MN10
MLDB — the standard English convention
SuttaCentral MN10
dhammatalks.org MN10

citations.short: Bhikkhu Bodhi: "contemplating the body in the body" (kāyānupassī)
citations.excerpt: MLDB MN10 — Bodhi preserves the doubled-noun construction (kāye kāyānupassī = 'contemplating body in body')

citations.short: Bhikkhu Sujato: "meditates by observing an aspect of the body" (kāyānupassī)
citations.excerpt: SuttaCentral MN10 (bilara-verified) — Sujato unpacks the kāye + kāyānupassī construction as 'aspect of body' for clarity

citations.short: Ṭhānissaro Bhikkhu: "remains focused on the body in & of itself" (kāyānupassī)
citations.excerpt: dhammatalks.org MN10 — Thanissaro renders the locative construction as 'in & of itself' to emphasise the non-dualistic framing
MLDB MN10 — Bodhi's standard rendering across the satipaṭṭhāna formula
SuttaCentral MN10 (bilara-verified) — Sujato's 'keen, aware, and mindful' formula
dhammatalks.org MN10 — Thanissaro's 'ardent, alert, & mindful' formula. Same rendering as Bodhi here.
Wikipedia article on ātāpī as the quality of effort in satipaṭṭhāna practice.
MLDB MN10 — Bodhi's standard rendering, preserves the precision-of-knowing emphasis
SuttaCentral MN10 (bilara-verified) — Sujato's 'keen, aware, and mindful' formula
dhammatalks.org MN10 — Thanissaro's 'ardent, alert, & mindful' formula
Wikipedia article on sampajañña (the abstract noun form) — the quality of clear comprehension in mindfulness practice.
MLDB — Bodhi's standard. Preserves Western philosophical tradition's 'feeling' but flag the technical narrowness in his footnotes.
SuttaCentral MN10 (bilara-verified) — Sujato also uses 'feeling'. 'observing an aspect of feelings' for vedanānupassī.
dhammatalks.org MN10 — 'remains focused on feelings in & of themselves' for vedanānupassī.
Wikipedia article on vedanā as the second of the five aggregates; the three feeling-tones (pleasant, painful, neutral).
MLDB MN10. Standard rendering — preserves the cognitive-faculty sense.
SuttaCentral MN10 (bilara-verified) — 'observing an aspect of the mind' for cittānupassī.
dhammatalks.org MN10 — 'remains focused on the mind in & of itself' for cittānupassī.
Wikipedia article distinguishing citta from mano and viññāṇa in Pāli psychology.
MLDB MN10. Bodhi's earlier choice; later editions sometimes shift to 'phenomena'.
SuttaCentral MN10 (bilara-verified) — 'observing an aspect of principles' for dhammānupassī.
dhammatalks.org MN10 — 'remains focused on mental qualities in & of themselves' for dhammānupassī.
Wikipedia article on the multiple senses of dhamma/dharma — teaching, phenomenon, mental quality, ultimate law.

tooltips: evaṁ means 'thus; in this way'. It opens the formula 'evaṁ me sutaṁ' (thus I have heard), signalling 'what follows is recited from memory'.
Pronounced as a soft nasal close — like the 'um' in English 'hum' or 'sum'.

senses.nuance: Narrative-opening deictic ('in this way') — points forward to what is about to be recited
senses.notes: DPD treats evaṁ and eva as distinct headwords. evaṁ ('thus; in this way; similarly; in the same manner') is the cataphoric deictic used in this formula; bare eva carries the emphatic-particle senses ('only', 'just', 'indeed').

tooltips: Form is 'of me' (genitive case); function here is 'by me' — Pāli uses the genitive to mark who did the action when the verb is passive, like 'was heard'.
'su' is the root meaning 'to hear'. The full present-tense form is suṇāti — 'hears'.
More specifically: the act of receiving and remembering teaching.
The -ta ending puts the verb in completed form — 'has been heard'. Pāli uses one shape for both 'heard' (the act) and 'what was heard' (the content).
The -ṁ closes 'sutaṁ' with the same form for two readings: 'was heard' (verb-like) and 'what was heard' (noun-like). English needs different phrasings; Pāli holds both at once.
Read with 'me' before it, 'me sutaṁ' compresses 'by me' + 'was heard' into two Pāli words. English needs scaffolding like 'have' and 'I' to say the same thing.
'eka' means 'one' or 'a certain' — it picks out a specific one of the noun that follows (here: samayaṁ, 'occasion').
Tells you which (one specific occasion), not what kind.
The -ṁ on 'ekaṁ' is a time-marker. Pāli puts -ṁ on the word for time (where English would say 'at' or 'on'). Same ending appears on 'samayaṁ' next.
It looks like a small word on its own, but it attaches to a root that follows (here: 'aya'). The full meaning emerges from prefix + root + suffix.
'aya' comes from the root 'i' meaning 'to go'.
As a noun-form it means 'going' or 'a course'.
'sam' (together) + 'aya' (going) = 'a coming together' — a moment when things converge: an occasion.
Same time-marker as on 'ekaṁ' above — together they say 'at one occasion'. Pāli marks the time-word with -ṁ rather than a separate preposition.
'bhaga' means fortune, good luck — a blessing.
From the root 'bhaj' meaning 'to share, distribute' — what is shared / what one is given.
The -vā suffix means 'one who has' or 'is endowed with' — bhaga (fortune) + -vā = 'the fortunate one'.
'The Fortunate One' — a respectful name for the Buddha, used through the canon.
'kurū' is the stem meaning 'Kuru' — a tribal people from the Kuru kingdom in ancient north India.
The vowel gets stretched (kuru → kurū) before the ending. Pāli often lengthens vowels at word-joins like this.
One of the 16 'Great Nations' (mahājanapada) of the Buddha's time — the cultural map he lived in.
The -su ending means 'among the' — Pāli marks 'place where' on the noun itself, with no separate preposition like English 'in' or 'among'.
'Among the ' — the dwelling-place of what the verb describes next.
'vi' is a prefix meaning 'apart' or 'asunder' — like English 'a-' in 'apart'.
Combined with the root 'hṛ' (carry/hold), the meaning shifts from 'to carry' to 'to dwell' — hold oneself apart in a place.
'hṛ' is a root meaning 'to carry' or 'to hold'.
With the prefix 'vi-' attached: 'to dwell, to abide' (vihar-).
Also: 'to live' or 'to conduct oneself' — staying somewhere over time.
The 'a' is a connector vowel that lets 'vihar' join the personal ending '-ti' that follows. Pāli often inserts this 'a' to keep words flowing smoothly.
The -ti ending says the subject is 'he', 'she', or 'it' — Pāli marks the person on the verb itself, no separate pronoun needed.
Pāli tells past stories in the present tense: viharati ('dwells') reads in English as 'was dwelling'.
'Kammāsa' means spotted or speckled — like a leopard's coat.
In folklore, also the name of a man-eating ogre (a fearsome flesh-eater).
The Jātaka tales (Buddhist birth stories) tell of King Kammāsapāda — 'the spotted-footed king' — who was tamed in this region.
Likely from a root 'dam' meaning 'to tame, subdue'.
The full place-name encodes the story: 'Kammāsa-dhamma' = 'where the spotted-one was tamed'.
Spelled with 'dh' here in the Sanskrit-influenced style — different from the more common Pāli 'Dhamma' (teaching).
The -ṁ closes the place-name 'Kammāsadhammaṁ' — Pāli marks names with the same dot-m ending used for other singular forms.
Tells you this is the name OF the town that follows. The 'nāma' word right after will spell out the link explicitly.
The same form is used for both 'the X' (subject) and 'X' (object) for neuter nouns — Pāli leaves this ambiguous; the X-nāma-Y construction doesn't disambiguate.

senses.notes: Traditional/commentarial explanation associated with the Kammāsapāda story; not directly attested by the DPD lexical entry, which only gives the proper noun.
Compound parse: kammāsa (spotted/speckled) + dhamma (here in a derived sense, possibly 'practice/place'). The standard morphological breakdown; not the historical/Jātaka etymology above.

tooltips: 'nāma' is the naming-word. 'X nāma Y' reads as 'Y called X' — the small word in the middle does the work of English 'called' or 'named'.
Here it links the proper name (Kammāsadhamma) to the common-noun head (nigamo, 'market town'): 'a market-town of the Kurus named Kammāsadhamma'.

senses.notes: The naming particle, indeclinable. DPD: 'called; means; by the name of; namely'. The X-nāma-Y appositional construction.

tooltips: Same Kuru people as in 'kurūsu' before — the inhabitants of the Kuru kingdom.
The vowel is stretched (kuru → kurū) before the ending. Pāli often lengthens vowels at word-joins like this.
The -naṁ ending means 'of the ' — Pāli marks 'belonging to' on the noun itself (where English would say 'of the').
'Of the Kurus' — their town. Same stem as in phase-c's 'kurūsu' but with a different ending: -su (among) vs -naṁ (of).
Sets up the meaning: the place people 'go down to' (the root that follows is 'gam', 'to go').
ni + gam = 'going down to' — the place where people resort, often for trade and gathering.
The -o ending marks this word as the subject of the sentence — 'a town' (the thing being named).
'A town' — what the whole phrase is really about. Everything else (Kammāsadhammaṁ, nāma, kurūnaṁ) describes which town and whose.

senses.notes: DPD's literal gloss 'going down' (ni+gam) supports the etymological reading already in d4s1/d4s2 tooltips — town as 'place people resort to'.
DPD attests 'town; market town' only; the 'commercial center' framing is a curatorial expansion drawing on standard Pāli economic context (nigama vs gāma vs nagara). Not directly DPD-attested.

tooltips: 'ta' is the demonstrative stem — the part meaning 'that'. By itself it's just the pointer; the next part (-tra) adds 'in that place'.
The -tra suffix means 'in that place'. ta (that) + -tra (place) = tatra = 'there'.
Built like English 'there' = th- (pointer) + -ere (place). Same morpheme-stacking, different sounds.
'kho' is an emphatic particle, like English 'indeed', 'now', or 'so' inserted to mark the rhythm of a story.
From an older Pāli word 'khalu' — both mean 'indeed' or 'truly'. The shorter 'kho' is the canonical form in the suttas.
Often left untranslated in English. Pāli wraps narrative timing in these little words; English uses pauses, punctuation, or simply omits them.

senses.notes: DPD attests 'kho' as 'indeed; surely; certainly; truly'. The choice to leave it untranslated in many English versions reflects a translator's curatorial decision — English narrative doesn't need this particle.

tooltips: 'bhaga' means fortune, good luck — a blessing.
From the root 'bhaj' meaning 'to share, distribute' — what is shared / what one is given.

Highlighted in a recurring color because 'bhagavā' is a refrain — the Buddha-epithet appears throughout the canon. The matching underline color marks the recurrence (you saw it back in phase-b's 'Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā…').
The -vā suffix means 'one who has' or 'is endowed with'. bhaga (fortune) + -vā = 'the fortunate one'.
'The Fortunate One' / 'The Blessed One' — a respectful name for the Buddha, used through the canon.

senses.notes: Compositional reading from bhaga (fortune) + -vā (one-who-has). DPD's primary glosses are 'Sublime/Blessed/Fortunate One'; the bare 'Lucky' captures the etymological flavor but isn't a standard rendering.

tooltips: 'bhikkh' is the root, related to 'begging' — but specifically religious mendicancy, not poverty.
Connected to the root 'bhaj' (to share) — a bhikkhu both receives alms and shares the Buddha's teaching.
Names one who lives on alms — a monastic dependent on lay support for food.
The -ū ending makes it plural. The same form serves multiple roles (subject pl, object pl, vocative pl) — only context tells you which.
Here 'bhikkhū' is the object: who the Buddha is addressing. The case is technically called accusative.

senses.notes: 'Mendicant' is the literal English match: one who depends on alms. DPD includes 'mendicant' among its glosses.

tooltips: The 'ā-' prefix means 'towards' — directs the verb's action at someone.
Intensifies the speaking: not just saying, but speaking AT or TO.
'mant' is the root meaning 'to think, counsel, advise'.
The same root that gives us 'mantra' — a counseled word, a verbal formula. Pedagogically: the Buddha 'mantra-d' the monks, gave them his counsel.
Used here as a verbal root, not a noun. The full compound (ā-mant-e-si) means 'addressed' or 'called out to'.
The 'e' between root and ending is a causative marker — turns 'to counsel' into 'to cause to counsel', which slides into 'to address' or 'to summon'.
The -si ending marks past tense — 'he/she/it did'. Specifically aorist (a real past), different from Pāli's storytelling-present (compare 'viharati' back in phase-c).
'he addressed' — completed action, situated in narrative time. The Buddha did this thing, then-and-there.
Same root as 'bhikkhū' in phase-e — religious mendicant, 'one who lives on alms'.

Highlighted in the same recurring color as 'bhikkhū' in phase-e — both are forms of bhikkhu. Matching color across phases marks the refrain (you'll see this stem every time the Buddha addresses the monks).
The -avo ending is vocative plural — used when calling out to a group. Like English 'O ____!' or 'Hey, ____!'
Phase-e had 'bhikkhū' (the monks the Buddha was addressing, accusative); phase-f has 'Bhikkhavo' (the same monks, now being called directly, vocative). Same noun, different role.

senses.notes: DPD attests the lemma bhikkhu (dpd:49885); the vocative-plural surface 'Bhikkhavo' is a dialectal variant of 'bhikkhave' which DPD does enumerate. Both forms address the same monastic audience.

tooltips: Shortened from 'iti' meaning 'thus' or 'in this way' — Pāli marks quoted speech by saying 'thus' after it: 'X-iti' = '"X"'.
Cross-language pattern: English uses quote marks (typographic); Pāli uses a word (lexical). Same function, different surface.
'bhad' carries a sense of good, auspicious, fortunate.
Related to 'bhadra' (fortunate) — the same root family that names blessings and noble persons.

The -ante ending is vocative singular — calling out to one person, respectfully. Pāli's '-e' marks vocative for many noun-classes (parallel to the '-avo' on Bhikkhavo in phase-f, which was vocative plural).
Used as a formulaic honorific: when monks address their teacher, this is the canonical response-word.

senses.notes: DPD's Lookup table doesn't index 'Bhadante' specifically (a coverage gap, like 'Bhikkhavo' in phase-f); grounded in the bhadanta lemma + standard Pāli vocative morphology.
Compositional reading: bhad (good) + -ante (vocative). The literal sense behind the honorific.
An English-register equivalent that captures the deferential tone — used as an alternative when 'Sir!' feels too informal.

tooltips: The same iti / ti quote-closer you saw in phase-f after 'Bhikkhavo'. Pāli marks the END of quoted speech with this word; here it closes the monks' reply ('Sir!').
Shortened from 'iti' (thus). Cross-language: English uses typographic quote marks; Pāli uses a lexical particle.
'te' here points back to the monks the Buddha just addressed: 'those (monks)'. Pāli has rich pronoun forms (te has 8 different DPD senses for different cases/persons) — context picks which.
Built on the same demonstrative stem as 'tatra' (phase-e: 'there') — Pāli uses one pointer-system for both place and person.
Same root as 'bhikkhū' in phase-e and 'Bhikkhavo' in phase-f — religious mendicant, 'one who lives on alms'.

Watch this: 'bhikkhū' has appeared in THREE different cases across the last three phases — phase-e bhikkhū-accusative ('addressed the monks'), phase-f Bhikkhavo-vocative ('Monks!' direct call), phase-g bhikkhū-nominative ('the monks replied'). Same noun, three roles, the surface form 'bhikkhū' is identical in two of them.
Here it's the SUBJECT — the monks doing the replying. Pāli marks subject with the nominative case; for u-stem masc plurals like bhikkhu, the nom-pl surface is 'bhikkhū' (long ū).
'bhaga' means fortune, good luck — the blessing root (you saw this stem in phase-b and phase-e's 'bhagavā').
From the root 'bhaj' meaning 'to share, distribute' — what is shared / what one is given.

Compare to phase-b/e: there it was 'bhagavā' (nominative — the Buddha doing/being the subject). Here it's 'bhagavato' (genitive — 'of the Buddha' or, in this construction, 'to the Buddha' as recipient of the reply). Same stem, different role.
The -vato ending marks genitive singular. For verbs of speaking/replying, Pāli's genitive often slides into a 'to whom' meaning — DPD attests both 'of the Buddha' and 'to the Buddha' as readings.
The arrow to the verb (Replied TO) marks this dative-like function: the monks replied AT/TO the Buddha.

senses.notes: The genitive surface 'bhagavato' has multiple readings in DPD (to/of/from). For verbs of speaking the dative-like 'to' reading is conventional; the bare 'of' would translate as 'they did the replying-of the Buddha' which is awkward English but grammatically possible.

tooltips: 'pacc' is the prefix 'paṭi' meaning 'back' or 'in return' — same as English 're-' in 'reply' or 'respond'.
'paṭi' becomes 'pacc' before a vowel-beginning element (paṭi + ass → pacc-ass). Pāli's sandhi rules smooth the join.
Built on the root 'su' meaning 'to hear' — the same root you saw in phase-a's 'sutaṁ' (heard).
Compound logic: paṭi (back) + su (hear) → 'to hear back' → 'to reply'. The reply IS hearing-back, in Pāli's mental world.
The -suṁ ending is aorist 3rd plural — 'they did this in the past'. Compare phase-e's āmantesi (aorist 3rd SINGULAR, 'he addressed'); here it's the plural answering: 'they replied'.

senses.notes: DPD's primary glosses are 'replied; agreed; assented'. 'Acknowledged' captures the receptive quality — hearing-back means accepting the call.

tooltips: 'bhaga' means fortune, good luck — the blessing root (you saw this stem in phase-b, phase-e, phase-g).
From the root 'bhaj' meaning 'to share, distribute' — what is shared / what one is given.

Fourth appearance of the bhagavā stem in the corpus. The form has cycled: phase-b/e nom-sg (subject) → phase-g gen-sg 'bhagavato' (recipient of reply) → phase-h nom-sg again (back to subject). Same Buddha-epithet, three roles.
Here it's back to nominative: the Buddha as the subject doing the action (saying). The -vā ending marks 'one who has' (the Fortunate One) + nominative singular masculine.
Compare to phase-g where the monks replied 'to him' (bhagavato). Now he speaks (Bhagavā). The conversation alternates speakers; the case-marking tracks the role.

senses.notes: Compositional reading from bhaga (fortune) + -vā (one-who-has). Same etymological-sense pattern as phase-e e3.senses[2].

tooltips: Points FORWARD to what comes next: 'said THIS' — meaning the actual teaching the Buddha is about to speak. The reader's anticipation is encoded grammatically.
More emphatic than the simpler form 'tad'. The extra 'e-' at the start is an attention-marker: 'THIS, specifically' rather than just 'that thing'.

senses.notes: DPD's Lookup table doesn't index 'etad' as a surface form (a coverage gap, like 'Bhadante' phase-g and 'Bhikkhavo' phase-f). The lemma 'eta' / 'etad' is the standard demonstrative stem; the neuter-sg-nom/acc form is grammatically attested across Pāli grammars.

tooltips: The 'a-' at the front is the augment — Pāli's way of marking 'this happened in the past'. Pre-pended to the verb-stem to make it past-tense.
Same letter that opens past-tense verbs in Sanskrit and Greek (the Greek augment is also 'a-'). Indo-European family resemblance — these languages share a past-marking ancestor.
'vac' is the root meaning 'to speak, say'.
The same root behind Latin 'vox' (voice) and English 'voice', 'vocal'. Indo-European: same speech-act, multiple languages.
The final '-a' is the aorist 3rd-singular ending — 'he/she/it did this'. Third aorist verb in the opening (cf. phase-e āmantesi -si, phase-g paccassosuṁ -suṁ).
Pattern: aorist endings vary by verb class. Compare: āmantesi (-si), avoca (-a), paccassosuṁ (-suṁ). Same tense, different stem-classes use different endings.

senses.notes: Lemma is 'vac' (to speak); avoca is the aorist 3sg with augment (a- + voc + -a). DPD's Lookup doesn't index 'avoca' directly (coverage gap; same shape as 'etad' and 'Bhadante') but the morphological pattern is grammatically standard.
English-register choice — 'declared' captures the gravity of the Buddha's speech-act in a discourse-opener context.

tooltips: 'ek' means 'one' or 'single' — but in compounds the sense bends: 'alone', 'unified', 'unique', or 'converging to one'.
Same root as 'ekaṁ' in phase-b ('one occasion'). Here it's the first element of one of the most-debated compounds in the Pāli canon.
From the root 'i' meaning 'to go' (same root as 'aya' in phase-b's 'samaya' — a 'going-together' = occasion). 'āyana' is the noun-form: 'a going', 'a way', or 'a goal'.

The compound 'ekāyana' has multiple legitimate readings: ek + āyana = 'one-going' OR 'going to one place' OR 'going alone'. Pāli translators disagree on which is meant — the senses below cycle through the major interpretations.
This single word generates more translator debate than almost any other in MN10. Cycling through the senses shows the range; choose the one that resonates.
The -o ending marks masc nom sg — same as on 'maggo' (p4s2) below. Pāli adjectives agree with their nouns in case/number/gender; the matching -o endings tell you 'Ekāyano' modifies 'maggo'.

senses.notes: Sujato's translation choice. 'Direct path' frames the compound as 'going-straight-to-one-goal'. Most common English rendering in 21st-century translations.
Older translations sometimes choose 'one way' — emphasizing that this is THE method, no alternative. Bhikkhu Anālayo discusses this reading at length; the exclusive interpretation is controversial.
Bhikkhu Bodhi has favored 'the path one walks alone' — emphasizing the meditative interior dimension. Less common but defended by serious scholars.

An interpretive reading: 'all paths converge at this one'. Less textually grounded but pedagogically interesting — captures the 'satipaṭṭhāna is what every practice secretly aims at' framing some commentators favor.

The most exclusivist reading — 'THE only way'. Doctrinally controversial: it would make satipaṭṭhāna the sole liberation-path, excluding other Buddhist methods. Most modern translators avoid this in favor of 'direct'.

tooltips: Built on the demonstrative stem 'ima'; the surface form 'ayaṁ' is the masc/fem nom-sg. Same pointer-system as 'etad' (phase-h, neuter cataphoric) and 'te' (phase-g, masc pl anaphoric) — different cases and numbers.

Agrees with 'maggo' (the path) — both are masc nom sg. The agreement signals 'THIS path' (not 'this thing in general'). Pāli's case-marking lets the demonstrative travel; English needs explicit word order.
Same root as phases e/f/g — religious mendicant. Fifth appearance of this stem in the corpus; the Buddha keeps returning to address his monastic audience.

Highlighted in the recurring 'bhikkhu' refrain color — same audience-marker you've seen across phase-e (bhikkhū-accusative), phase-f (Bhikkhavo-vocative, alternate form), phase-g (te bhikkhū-nominative). Five appearances now; the refrain is solidly established.

The -ave ending is vocative plural — the canonical form for direct address to a group. Phase-f used the alternative 'Bhikkhavo'; this 'Bhikkhave' is the more common Pāli form. Sujato uses this throughout MN10.

Stylistic interjection: the Buddha addresses the monks AGAIN here (he's been speaking to them for two phases already). The vocative is rhetorical — it underlines the importance of what's about to come.

senses.notes: Compositional reading from the bhaj-share root etymology. Not a standard English rendering; included to surface the etymology pedagogically.
From the alternative etymology bhī-kkh (one-who-sees-danger). DPD glosses bhikkhu as 'beggar' primarily but the danger-seer etymology has a long tradition in commentary.
An interpretive choice some modern translators (e.g., Thanissaro Bhikkhu) favor — emphasizing the relational warmth over the formal monastic status. Not DPD-attested but pedagogically interesting.

tooltips: 'magga' is the central word for any way, road, or path in Pāli — especially the Buddha's eightfold path (aṭṭhaṅgika magga). DPD glosses it primarily as 'road; path; track; way'.
Related to Sanskrit 'mārga'; cognate with English 'mark' and 'march' (paths marked by walking, advance along a route).
Also takes derived senses: 'way; means; method' — a magga is not just a physical road but ANY systematic approach to a goal.
The -o ending marks masc nom sg — matching 'Ekāyano' (p1s3) and 'ayaṁ' (p2s1) above. The three together: 'THIS direct PATH' (with case-marker agreement on all three).

senses.notes: DPD attests 'magga' also as 'way; means; method' — the abstract sense that frames satipaṭṭhāna as a meditation METHOD, not just a physical path.

tooltips: The root sat- / sant- means 'being' or 'existing' — same family as Sanskrit sat. From this comes sattā, 'a being' or 'sentient creature'.

Some commentators invoke alternate root √saj 'to cling, to attach' to give sattā as 'attached ones' — etymologically defensible, doctrinally suggestive (beings ARE the attached, in Buddhist analysis), but not the mainstream lexical reading.

Cross-phase note: sattā stands in deliberate contrast to bhikkhū (monks, addressed in phases e-g). The Buddha frames the path as FOR all beings — wider than the monastic audience listening. This first explicit widening in the formula.
The -ānaṁ ending is genitive plural — 'of the '. Pāli marks the 'belongs-to' relationship on the noun itself, where English uses the preposition 'of'.
Same suffix appears in phase-3 on sokaparideva-ānaṁ ('of grief and lamentation'). The genitive-of-purpose pattern recurs throughout the satipaṭṭhāna formula.

senses.notes: Standard DPD-attested reading. Sujato, Bodhi, Thanissaro all use 'beings' as the default rendering.

From alternate root √saj 'to cling'. Pedagogically interesting because it captures the Buddhist analysis of beings AS those bound by attachment. Rarely chosen as the primary rendering but worth knowing.

satta also means the number seven (cognate of Sanskrit sapta). Homonym, NOT the intended reading here. Included to illustrate that Pāli surface forms genuinely have multiple lexical entries — students will encounter satta meaning 'seven' in other contexts.

tooltips: vi- is a perfective / intensifying prefix — roughly 'thoroughly', 'completely', 'apart'. Combined with √sudh 'be pure', it gives visuddhi: not just 'pure' but 'pure-through-and-through'.
Same prefix appears in vipassanā ('clear-seeing', insight) and viññāṇa ('consciousness'). Pāli students learn vi- as a productive intensifier early.
The root √sudh means 'be pure, be clean, be clear'. The derived noun suddhi captures both senses — 'purity' as a state, 'cleansing' as a process. Pāli abstract nouns straddle state and process.

Cognate of Sanskrit śuddha 'pure'. The familiar word Buddha-śuddhi ('purification of the Buddha-quality') is the same root. Not directly cognate with English 'pure' (different IE root) but the semantic field is parallel.
The -yā ending is dative-singular feminine — 'for the purpose of'. Pāli's dative-of-purpose marks WHY something is done; English uses 'for' as a preposition.
Parallel structure: phase-3's samatikkamāya ('for the surmounting') uses the same dative-of-purpose construction. The satipaṭṭhāna formula chains five such datives, each naming what the path is FOR.

senses.notes: Sujato, Ñāṇamoli-Bodhi, and most modern translators. Treats visuddhi as the activity / process — the path leads to PURIFYING. Standard reading.
Thanissaro often renders as 'purity' — treating visuddhi as the achieved state rather than the activity. Both are defensible since Pāli abstract nouns straddle state/process; the choice is editorial.

Less common but valid: visuddhi shares root with words for 'shine, brighten'. Connects pedagogically to vipassanā (clear-seeing) and the insight tradition's vocabulary. Choose this rendering if reading MN10 as primarily an insight text.

Preserves the concrete ritual sense — the Pāli verb for ritual washing uses √sudh. Translators who want the embodied physicality of the metaphor sometimes use this. Less common in modern translations.

Rooted in √sudh's secondary sense of brightness/clarity. Less anchored in canonical usage; included to show the metaphorical range available. Visarga in luminous-mind suttas (pabhassara citta) uses different vocabulary, but the family overlaps.

Process-of-refining metaphor (like refining gold). Modern English interpretation; less attested in classical commentaries but pedagogically vivid for readers familiar with the metallurgical metaphor in other religious traditions.

paliWords.pronunciation: vi · SUD · dhi · YAH  (rhymes with "ya" in "yard")

tooltips: From the root √suc 'to burn, to grieve' — Sanskrit cognate śoka. The Pāli noun captures grief specifically as an inward burning, distinct from outwardly visible mourning.
Same root family as Pāli sokin 'one who grieves' and sokahata 'grief-struck'. Inner-experience word.
Compound: pari- (around, all sides) + √div / √dev (lament, wail, cry-out). 'Lamentation' as the outward, vocal expression of grief — what others see and hear.

soka + parideva pair the inner (grief) and outer (lamentation) dimensions. The Buddha uses this dual structure repeatedly — also in the standard 'soka, parideva, dukkha, domanassa, upāyāsa' list of five afflictions.
The -ānaṁ ending is genitive plural — 'of the '. Same suffix as phase-2's sattānaṁ ('of beings'): Pāli marks the 'belongs-to' relationship on the noun where English uses 'of'.
Plural because soka and parideva together form a dvanda compound (one-and-the-other); the plural reads as 'of grief-and-lamentation'.

senses.notes: Sujato, Ñāṇamoli-Bodhi, Thanissaro all use this pair. The inner-outer dvanda is canonical and unambiguous.
Preserves the visceral physicality — soka as 'sorrow' (the heart-feeling), parideva as 'wailing' (the literal sound). Less common but pedagogically vivid for readers who want the embodied register.
Strict etymological reading — soka from √suc 'burn', parideva from √div 'cry'. Captures the metaphor before it crystallized into 'grief'. Not a translation choice; an etymological gloss.

paliWords.pronunciation: so · ka · pa · ri · DEH · vah · num

tooltips: The prefix sam- means 'together, completely, thoroughly' — adds a sense of totality or completion to the verb root. Cognate with Latin com- / con-.
Same prefix appears in samādhi ('concentration, gathering-together') and saṅgha ('the gathered ones').
The prefix ati- means 'across, beyond, over' — adds a sense of transgression or going-past. Cognate with Greek anti- and Latin trans-.
Same prefix appears in atikkanta ('gone past', often of time) and atikkamati ('he/she goes beyond').
The root √kram means 'to step, walk, stride'. Doubled here (-kkam-) as the Pāli verbal-stem doubling for past forms.
sam- + ati- + √kram together: 'completely-going-beyond' = surmounting / transcending. The verb stages the path's overcoming as a stepping-across.

The -āya ending is dative-singular masculine — 'for the purpose of'. Same dative-of-purpose construction as phase-2's visuddhiyā ('for purification'). The satipaṭṭhāna formula chains five such datives.
atikkama is a masculine action-noun derived from the verb atikkamati; the masculine -āya ending agrees with the underlying gender.

senses.notes: Sujato's choice. Captures both the verticality (going-over) and the completeness (sam-). Most common in modern translations.
Ñāṇamoli-Bodhi and others. Less spatial, more action-focused. Both renderings are defensible; the choice depends on whether you want the spatial metaphor preserved.
Carries philosophical-Buddhist weight in English (transcendence as goal). Some translators avoid as overly loaded; others use it precisely for that weight. Choose based on audience.
ati (beyond) + kram (step) = 'stepping beyond'. Preserves the embodied metaphor before it became 'overcoming'. Pedagogically useful for first encounter; rarely chosen as the primary rendering.

paliWords.pronunciation: sa · ma · tik · KAH · mah · ya
duk · kha · do · ma · nas · SĀ · naṁ

tooltips: Dukkha — 'suffering' is the standard English rendering but flattens the word. The Pāli covers physical pain, mental anguish, existential unsatisfactoriness, and the structural friction of conditioned experience all at once.

Literal etymology: du- (bad, off) + kha (axle hole). A wheel whose axle hole is poorly bored doesn't turn smoothly. The metaphor is mechanical, not metaphysical — dukkha is the wobble in the wheel of experience, not a cosmic verdict.

Domanassa — mental dejection, specifically the painful FEELING-TONE of negative experience. Distinct from dukkha (which can be physical) and from soka (grief, in phase-3) — domanassa is the inner displeasure that accompanies pain.

Compound: du- (bad) + manas (mind). 'Bad-minded-ness'. Pairs structurally with somanassa (happiness, su- + manas), which doesn't appear in this formula but is its counterweight elsewhere in the canon.

The -ānaṁ ending is genitive plural — 'of the [plurals]'. Same suffix appears in phase-2's sattānaṁ ('of beings') and phase-3's sokaparidevānaṁ ('of grief-and-lamentation'). The genitive-of-purpose pattern is the spine of this formula.

Plural because dukkha and domanassa together form a dvanda compound (suffering-and-distress as a single composite). The plural reads as 'of suffering-and-distress[-both]' even though English would naturally use singular.

senses.notes: Sujato consistently pairs 'pain' for dukkha and 'distress' for domanassa in MN10. Preserves the inner/outer pairing while keeping register accessible.

Bhikkhu Bodhi's MLDB renders dukkha as 'suffering' (heavier philosophical weight) and domanassa often as 'grief' (overlapping with soka from phase-3). Some readers find the soka/domanassa boundary blurred under this choice.

A more functional register: foregrounds the FELT QUALITY (unsatisfactoriness, dispreference) over the philosophical weight. Less common in published translations; useful when teaching the Abhidhamma feeling-tone analysis where dukkha-domanassa pairs with sukha-somanassa.

tooltips: Attha — disappearance, setting, going-down. Same word used for the SUN setting (e.g., 'suriyo atthaṁ gacchati' = 'the sun goes to setting'). The metaphor is fading-from-view, not destruction.

Caveat for readers who know Sanskrit: this attha (going-down) is a different word from attha 'meaning' / 'purpose' (Sanskrit artha). Same surface form, different etymological root. In compounds like atthaṅgama, it's always the 'setting' sense.
Connecting nasal — the n of attha-n-gam assimilates to ṅ before the velar g- of gam. Pāli writes the assimilation explicitly where Sanskrit might leave it implicit.

Not a separate morpheme historically; it's the surface trace of sandhi (sound-change at morpheme boundaries). v11 split it out as its own segment for pedagogical clarity — defensible but not how traditional grammars parse it.
The root √gam means 'to go'. Cognate with English 'come' via PIE *gʷem-. Productive across the Pāli verbal system: gacchati (he goes), gata (gone), āgata (come), saṁgam (assembly, going-together).
atthaṅgama as a compound = 'going-to-disappearance'. The action is the SETTING ITSELF — not just the end-state but the movement into vanishing.

The -āya ending is dative-singular masculine — 'for the purpose of'. atthaṅgama is a masculine action-noun (one of three -gama derivatives that show up in this formula: atthaṅgama, adhigama, samatikkama).

Third dative-of-purpose in the formula chain. Phase-2's visuddhiyā ('for purification'), phase-3's samatikkamāya ('for surmounting'), and this one stack five 'for' statements that the path is directed toward. The formula's rhythm comes from this five-fold parallel.

senses.notes: Sujato and most contemporary translators. Preserves the sun-setting connotation. Reads naturally in English as the contemplative process: pain doesn't get fought — it's allowed to fade.
Bhikkhu Bodhi's preferred rendering. Slightly more philosophical — emphasises the impermanence framework (anicca → uppāda + vaya, arising + passing-away). Same denotation, different framing weight.

Some translators (notably Thanissaro in some contexts) use 'extinguishing' to foreground the connection to nibbāna ('extinction'). Stronger reading; risks over-determining the doctrine. The Pāli atthaṅgama is broader than nibbāna and applies to many fades, not just final liberation.

tooltips: Ñāya — the technical Buddhist term for 'system' or 'method', but with weight: in the commentaries (Visuddhimagga, Aṭṭhakathā), ñāya is glossed as the Noble Eightfold Path itself. So 'attainment of ñāya' reads as 'reaching the path'.

Root √ñā (to know) + ya (action-noun suffix). Literally 'a knowing' or 'the principle by which one knows'. Cognate with Sanskrit nyāya (logic, the rational system). In Pāli Buddhism the rational sense is preserved but anchored to soteriology — not abstract logic, but the logic of liberation.

The -ssa ending is genitive singular — 'of the [one thing]'. Marks the shift in the formula chain: phases 2-4 used -ānaṁ (genitive plural — of beings, of griefs, of sufferings, many things in the world). Phase-5 and phase-6 use -ssa (singular — of the path, of nibbāna, the one transcendent goal).

The grammatical number tracks the conceptual movement: from world-as-multiplicity (-ānaṁ) to path-as-unity (-ssa). The Buddha's vocabulary scaffolding does the work — readers feel the shift without being told.

senses.notes: Bhikkhu Bodhi (MLDB): 'true way' / 'method'. The systematic path-as-procedure. Verified rendering at suttacentral.net/mn10/en/bodhi.

Sujato (SuttaCentral MN10): 'system'. Echoes the cognate Sanskrit nyāya (logical system) while staying within Pāli Buddhism's soteriological frame. Verified rendering at suttacentral.net/mn10/en/sujato.
Thanissaro (dhammatalks.org MN10): 'the right method'. Aligns with his Thai Forest framing of practice-as-method. Verified rendering at dhammatalks.org/suttas/MN/MN10.html.

tooltips: Prefix adhi- meaning 'onto', 'toward', 'over and above'. Directional intensifier — turns simple 'going' (gam) into 'going-toward-and-reaching'. Same prefix in adhipati (overlord, one-who-stands-over), adhiṭṭhāna (determination, standing-upon), adhipaññā (higher wisdom).

In the soteriological lexicon, adhi- often marks the experiential/realized dimension (vs. mere theoretical understanding). adhi-gama (reaching) pairs with pari-yatti (taking-up, theoretical mastery) — the foundational adhigama/pariyatti pair distinguishes direct realization from textual learning.
The root √gam means 'to go'. Cognate with English 'come' via PIE *gʷem-. Productive across the Pāli verbal system: gacchati (he goes), gata (gone), āgata (come), saṁgam (assembly).

Third -gama compound in the formula. Phase-3 had samatikkama (sam+ati+kam, surmounting-going — going over and across). Phase-4 had atthaṅgama (attha+gam, setting-going — going into disappearance). Phase-5 has adhigama (adhi+gam, reaching-going — going up and onto). All three are masculine action-nouns from √gam; the rhythm of the formula partly comes from this -gama parallelism.

The -āya ending is dative-singular masculine — 'for the purpose of'. Adhigama is a masculine action-noun; -āya marks it as the goal-purpose of the practice. Fourth dative-of-purpose in the chain: phase-2's visuddhiyā ('for purification'), phase-3's samatikkamāya ('for surmounting'), phase-4's atthaṅgamāya ('for disappearance'), this phase's adhigamāya ('for attainment'), and phase-6's sacchikiriyāya ('for realization').
Five 'for' statements stacked. The rhythm is liturgical — when chanted in Pāli, the recurring -āya creates a meditative cadence that mirrors the directionality of practice itself.

senses.notes: Bhikkhu Bodhi's MLDB and most contemporary translators. Renders the directed-reaching sense cleanly. Reads naturally as the practical goal of the meditator: not vague aspiration but actual reaching.

Sujato (SuttaCentral): 'for realizing the system'. Foregrounds the experiential dimension over the bare 'getting'. Closer to how adhigama functions in the adhigama/pariyatti pair — direct realization vs. textual mastery.

Thanissaro Bhikkhu sometimes uses 'discovery' for adhigama — emphasises that what is reached was always there; the work is finding it, not constructing it. Doctrinally aligned with the standard line that nibbāna is unconditioned and timeless: it isn't produced by practice, only uncovered.

tooltips: Nibbāna — the goal-term of the entire Buddhist path. Literally 'blowing out' or 'extinguishing'. Prefix ni- (out, away) + √vā (to blow). The metaphor is a flame being extinguished — not destruction but cooling.

Buddhist physics-of-mind reading: the 'fires' that go out are the three unwholesome roots (lobha/dosa/moha — greed, hatred, delusion). Awakening is the cooling of these fires, not the annihilation of the person. Without the three-fires context, 'extinguishment' flattens into nihilism — which is exactly the misreading the early translators worried about and Bhikkhu Bodhi's choice to leave the word untranslated guards against.

The -ssa ending is genitive singular — 'of [the one thing]'. Closes the singular pattern that began in phase-5 (ñāyassa). The formula chain pivots from gen plural (-ānaṁ in phases 2-4: of beings, griefs, sufferings) to gen singular (-ssa in phases 5-6: of the path, of nibbāna). World→path→goal, multiplicity collapsing to unity.

Same -ssa form as phase-5. The grammatical rhyme closes the chain: ñāyassa adhigamāya / nibbānassa sacchikiriyāya. Two parallel datives-of-purpose marking the two faces of the goal — the path (ñāya, the means) and nibbāna (the end).

senses.notes: Bhikkhu Bodhi (MLDB) consistently leaves nibbāna untranslated, preserving its status as a technical term. Reasoning: any English word imports baggage (Christian 'salvation', nihilist 'extinction', Western 'liberation') that distorts the original. Leaving it in Pāli forces the reader to learn the word in its own context.

Sujato (SuttaCentral): 'extinguishment' — recovers the literal blowing-out metaphor. Pairs with his consistent rendering of the three fires as 'greed, hate, delusion'. The reader feels the metaphor working: awakening is cooling, not gaining.

Thanissaro Bhikkhu's signature rendering. Emphasises freedom-from-the-aggregates rather than cooling-of-fires. Doctrinally tied to the aggregates analysis: nibbāna as the release from grasping at form, feeling, perception, volition, consciousness. Less common in academic translations but distinctive in the Thai Forest tradition.

paliWords.pronunciation: sac · chi · ki · ri · YĀ · ya

tooltips: Sacchi — 'before-the-eyes', a transparent compound: sa- (with) + akkhi (eye). The word makes explicit what would be implicit in English: realization is EYE-WITNESSED, first-person, direct. Not derived from authority, scripture, or inference.

The epistemological keystone of the meditation tradition. Buddhist practice values direct knowing (paccatta-vedanīya — to-be-known-for-oneself) over reported knowing. sacchi- foregrounds this commitment in the formula's closing word.
Kiriya — from √kar (to do, make, perform). The action-form of 'making'. Combined with sacchi-, the compound sacchikiriyā = 'making-real-before-the-eyes' or 'rendering-witnessable'.

Same root underlies kamma (action, deed), kāra (maker), karoti (he does). The Pāli verbal system organises around √kar — sacchikiriyā situates nibbāna's realization within that action-frame, but with the twist that the action here is recognition, not construction.

The -āya ending is dative-singular feminine here — sacchikiriyā is feminine (the -ā stem). FIFTH and FINAL dative-of-purpose in the formula chain. The five together: visuddhiyā (purification), samatikkamāya (surmounting), atthaṅgamāya (disappearance), adhigamāya (attainment), sacchikiriyāya (realization). The formula CLOSES here.

Liturgical effect: when chanted, the recurring -āya across five phrases creates a five-fold meditative cadence. The Buddha's pedagogical engineering is audible — the structure of the practice is mirrored in the structure of the verse.

senses.notes: Bhikkhu Bodhi's MLDB and most contemporary translators. Captures the directness without philosophical baggage. Reads naturally in English as the contemplative moment of recognition.
Foregrounds the sacchi- (with-eye) component. Sometimes used in academic translations where the etymology matters pedagogically. Less common in published English suttas but doctrinally precise.

Thanissaro Bhikkhu often uses 'direct experiencing' for sacchikiriyā in some contexts — emphasises the first-person epistemic quality. Aligns with the Thai Forest tradition's stress on practice-knowledge over textual-knowledge.

tooltips: Yad — relative pronoun 'which' or 'that-which'. The y- ending of the relative stem. Cognate with Sanskrit yad, Latin id (idem, identical).
In compounds the final -d acts as a sandhi-linker before vowels — yad + idaṁ joins smoothly as yadidaṁ.
Idaṁ — demonstrative 'this' (neuter singular). Points forward, not back. The compound yadidaṁ thus means 'which-this' = 'namely' or 'that is to say', introducing a forthcoming enumeration.

Discourse marker function: signals to the reader that a list or specification is coming. The Pāli formula is precise about pointing — yadidaṁ specifically gestures FORWARD to what's about to be named.

senses.notes: Sujato, Bhikkhu Bodhi, and most contemporary translators. Functions as a colon in English — introduces the list.
Ñāṇamoli sometimes used this fuller form. Preserves the discourse-marker function explicitly. Slightly more old-fashioned register.

tooltips: Cattāro — the number four. Nominative plural masculine, agreeing with satipaṭṭhānā. Cognate with English four, Latin quattuor, Greek tessares, via PIE *kʷetwóres.

The number is not arbitrary. The four foundations (kāya / vedanā / citta / dhammā — body / feeling / mind / mental-objects) form a cognitive-meditative framework that subsequent phases of MN10 lay out one by one. This phase NAMES the four; phases 8 onward unpack each.

senses.notes: Most translators use 'the four' here because cattāro is followed by the named referents (satipaṭṭhānā). The definite article is supplied by English convention.
Functional equivalent. Loses the implied definiteness but matches the bare Pāli.

tooltips: Sati — mindfulness, presence, awareness. Cognate with Sanskrit smṛti (memory, remembrance). Root √smṛ (to remember). The Pāli sati keeps the memory-resonance: mindfulness as the faculty of REMEMBERING what is happening, KEEPING IN MIND.

Theravāda tradition glosses sati as 'apilāpana' — un-floating, keeping things from drifting away. The cognitive function is anti-distraction: holding the object of meditation in the field of awareness so it doesn't slip out. Christian translators reaching for 'mindfulness' borrowed this memory-emphasis explicitly.

Paṭṭhānā — the most contested half of the compound. Two parses: (1) paṭi + sthā = 'going-toward-and-standing' (foundation, establishment), or (2) upa + sthāna = 'close-attendance' (presencing, attending-near). The compound is doubled-tt because of the consonant assimilation, and that doubling masks which prefix was originally there.

Bhikkhu Anālayo's monograph defends the upaṭṭhāna parse on the grounds that paṭṭhāna appears only in Abhidhamma texts while upaṭṭhāna is readily visible in the Nikayas and Sanskrit parallels. Bhikkhu Bodhi PHILOLOGICALLY agrees with Anālayo (upaṭṭhāna parse) but his MLDB TRANSLATION keeps 'foundations of mindfulness' for continuity with prior English literature (Soma Thera, Ñāṇaponika). Sujato and Thanissaro follow the upaṭṭhāna parse in their renderings ('kinds of mindfulness meditation' and 'establishings of mindfulness' respectively). The parse decision and the translation choice are separable — don't conflate them.

senses.notes: Bhikkhu Bodhi (MLDB): 'foundations of mindfulness'. Bodhi's TRANSLATION CHOICE preserves the historical English convention (Soma Thera, Ñāṇaponika). His PHILOLOGICAL POSITION, per Wikipedia summarising Anālayo's argument, actually favors the upaṭṭhāna parse — but the established 'foundations' rendering is kept for continuity. Translation choice and parse position are separable decisions.

Sujato (SuttaCentral MN10): 'four kinds of mindfulness meditation'. His current rendering. Renders paṭṭhāna as a form of upa-sthāna (close-attendance), foregrounding meditation-as-practice over foundation-as-structure. Aligns with Anālayo's philological defense of the upaṭṭhāna parse.

Thanissaro Bhikkhu (dhammatalks.org MN10): 'establishings of mindfulness'. His current rendering, evolved from his earlier 'frames of reference' (Wings to Awakening era). The 'establishing' verbal-noun emphasises the active placing-of-attention process, aligning with the upaṭṭhāna parse.
